data.title: Compass Point starts coverage on CoreWeave with bullish rating
data.description:
Compass Point started coverage on CoreWeave (CRWV) with a Buy rating, saying that a boost in AI-driven data center spending and secured contracts from blue-chip clients strengthened conviction that the AI infrastructure provider’s growth is durable.

The brokerage set a PT of $150 on the stock, implying an upside of over 75% to the stock’s last close.

Analyst Michael Donovan said that the rising tide of investment in AI infrastructure provides a massive tailwind for CoreWeave and the company “is positioned to ride that wave as a leader in GPU cloud computing.”

“Though a relatively new public company—listed March 28, 2025 —CoreWeave already serves Microsoft and has captured long-term demand: revenue backlog reached $55.6B at the end of 3Q25, up 85% sequentially from $30.1B in 2Q25; backlog consists of $50.0B remaining performance obligations plus $5.6B of additional amounts expected under committed contracts, supported by clients like OpenAI with $22.4B committed and Meta with a $14.2B deal through 2031. Nvidia, a 7% shareholder, agreed to a $6.3B capacity backstop to ensure any unused GPUs continue to generate revenue through 2032,” Donovan noted.

CoreWeave was trading 5% lower on Thursday in afternoon trading. However, the stock has more than doubled in value so far this year, compared to the over 16% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street are bullish and rated the stock a Buy.

Earlier on Wednesday, CoreWeave signed an agreement to increase its revolving credit facility to $2.5B from $1.5B and extend the maturity date to November 2029 from May 2028.

CoreWeave reported its fiscal third-quarter results on Monday, which saw revenue surge 132.9% year-over-year to $1.36 billion.

data.title: ReNew to spend $9.3B on green energy projects in India's Andhra Pradesh state
data.description:
India's ReNew Energy (RNW) said Thursday it plans to spend ~820 billion rupees ($9.33 billion) in the state of Andhra Pradesh to expand its clean energy portfolio, as the country pushes to double its non-fossil fuel power generation capacity to 500 GW.

ReNew (RNW) said it will set up a 6 GW photovoltaic ingot-wafer plant, a 2 GW pumped hydro project, a 300,000 metric tons/year green ammonia facility, and 5 GW of hybrid renewable projects in the state, including wind-solar and solar-plus-storage.

The latest investment commitment includes 600 billion rupees in new investments and adds to the 220 billion rupees pledged in May for a hybrid renewable energy project, the company said, without describing how it will fund the projects.

ReNew (RNW), which already operates 717 MW of wind and 60 MW of solar capacity in Andhra Pradesh, said it aims to develop 78.5 GW of solar, 35 GW of wind and 25 GWh of battery storage capacity in the state.

data.title: From the 'Big Short' to the big mystery: A timeline of Michael Burry’s bold market plays
data.description:
Nearly two decades after he became a Wall Street legend for predicting the 2008 housing crash, investor Michael Burry once again came out of the shadows with his bold bets against AI darlings Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR).

However, in a surprise move, Burry's firm, Scion Asset Management, has deregistered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, effective November 10.

Here’s how one of the market’s most notorious contrarians got here—and why investors are watching closely: 

2004–2008: The “Big Short” era

Burry forayed into the big leagues in the mid-2000s by spotting the rot in America's housing market years before it collapsed. He had launched his hedge fund, Scion Capital, in November 2000. 

During the mid-2000s, through Scion Capital, he used credit-default swaps to bet against subprime mortgage bonds.

A report from Vanity Fair suggested that by the second quarter of 2005, Scion Capital had amassed over $1 billion in CDS exposure on subprime mortgage bonds, a bet against what Burry believed was a market waiting to implode.

His bet paid off massively as the market imploded in 2007-2008.

Burry had made his mark. Michael Lewis chronicled the story in his book The Big Short: Inside the Doomsday Machine. The book was later made into a movie named The Big Short, where actor Christian Bale played Burry. 

However, after the euphoria settled, Burry shut Scion Capital and took a break from managing other people's money.

Reports suggested he shut down the company in 2008, as he was flooded by lawsuits and IRS audits.

2013–2020: The return 

In 2013, Burry came back and reopened his company, this time in the name of Scion Asset Management. 

This time, his concern was about what he called the “index bubble.” In a 2019 interview with Bloomberg News, he compared the rise of index funds to the pre-2008 boom in subprime collateralized debt obligations (CDOs). He added passive investing has removed price discovery from the equity markets.

Burry had also reportedly built a huge position in GameStop (GME) before it became a massive meme stock and surged leaps and bounds. However, he had sold his entire stake in late 2020, thus missing out on the 2000% leap that GME saw at one point in 2021. 

2021: The Tesla gamble

In May 2021, filings revealed that Scion had taken large put-option positions against Tesla (TSLA), reflecting Burry’s belief that the EV maker’s valuation had become detached from fundamentals. A Reuters report said the company had put options on 800,100 shares in the Elon Musk-founded company as of the end of the first quarter in 2021.

Regarding GME's meteoric rise in 2021, Burry had reportedly tweeted in January, saying that the squeeze was “unnatural, insane and dangerous,” and called for legal and regulatory consequences for such trading. By this time, Burry had sold his position in GME. 

2025: The AI warnings

Burry, once again, seemed to keep a low profile. However, in the past few weeks, he returned to the limelight with his bold views on AI and tech companies. 

He also changed his profile name on X to Cassandra Unchained. Cassandra was a Trojan princess whose accurate predictions were doomed to be disbelieved.

October 31, 2025

Burry shared a photo of actor Christian Bale as Burry in a scene from the movie “The Big Short” and wrote, “Sometimes we see bubbles. Sometimes, there is something to do about it. Sometimes, the only winning move is not to play.”

His posts prior to October 31 appeared to be deleted.

November 4, 2025

Last week, Burry’s Scion Asset Management took bearish positions in AI darlings Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR), as he hinted at caution toward the artificial intelligence trade that has boosted a lot of this year's market rally. He also disclosed new bullish positions in Pfizer (PFE), Halliburton (HAL), Molina Healthcare (MOH), and Lululemon Athletica (LULU).

Soon enough, Palantir (PLTR) CEO Alex Karp hit back and criticized short sellers while calling out Burry. “The two companies he’s shorting are the ones making all the money, which is super weird,” Karp told CNBC. “The idea that chips and ontology are what you want to short is bats--- crazy,” Karp said.

November 10, 2025

However, that did not change Burry's views. On Tuesday, Burry sounded a new alarm, targeting Big Tech's accounting practices. He said that major cloud and AI infrastructure companies are artificially inflating profits by extending the “useful life” of their assets. He had named big companies, including Oracle (ORCL), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) in a post on X.

In the same post, he said more details will be revealed on November 25.

His current X bio reads, “Official X account for 'The Big Short'. Michael Burry, M.D., dubbed 'Cassandra' by Warren Buffett. Now unchained—??? launches Nov 25th, Stay Tuned!”

November 13, 2025

An SEC filing and a post on Burry's X feed confirmed that Scion Asset Management has terminated its registration status with the Securities and Exchange Commission, effective November 10.

Several posts on X circulated a photo of a letter dated October 27 from Burry to Scion's investors saying that he was winding down his fund.

November 25, 2025

In his latest post, Burry once again alluded to the November 25 date and said, “On to much better things November 25.”

Burry has offered no further clarification on what “November 25” signifies, leaving investors guessing.

More related stories

  • Michael Burry accuses tech giants of inflating profits by stretching asset lifespans
  • Palantir CEO Alex Karp slams ‘Big Short’ investor Burry for betting against Palantir, Nvidia
  • Michael Burry adds bullish bets on Pfizer, Halliburton, Molina, and Lululemon
  • Michael Burry’s ‘Big Short’ instincts target AI trade with puts on Nvidia, Palantir

data.title: Kneat.com signals continued SaaS momentum and outlines 20–30% EBITDA margin target while expanding AI and R&D initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: kneat.com, inc. (KSI:CA) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Edmund Ryan, CEO, emphasized that "the third quarter of 2025 reaffirmed Kneat's resilience as the market leader in digital validation." He reported SaaS revenue growth of 33% year-over-year, highlighting demand for the platform despite extended buying cycles in the life sciences sector. Ryan pointed to a robust sales pipeline for Q4 and beyond, stating, "Kneat wins because it delivers compelling value for its customers... our validation workflow platform, Kneat Gx, is configurable and absolutely no coding is needed." He underscored a G2 satisfaction score of 98 out of 100 for Kneat, 20 points above its nearest competitor. The CEO reiterated ongoing strategic investments in R&D and go-to-market, aligned with a focus on profitability for the coming year.

  • CFO Dave O'Reilly said, "I'm pleased to report that Kneat's healthy growth continued in the third quarter with annual recurring revenue up 37%, total revenue up 26% and gross profit up 25%." O'Reilly detailed that revenue reached $16.1 million and SaaS license revenue was $15.2 million for the quarter. He noted operating expenses grew 43% year-on-year to $14.3 million, with R&D up 47%, sales and marketing up 45%, and G&A up 32%. O'Reilly concluded, "We ended the quarter with total annual recurring revenue, or ARR, of $68.6 million, up 37% from $49.9 million as at September 30, 2024. And our cash position as at September 30, 2025, was $59.8 million."

Outlook

  • Management indicated that while macroeconomic factors are extending buying cycles and causing some deal slippage, the sales pipeline remains strong and expansions are expected to accelerate toward year-end. Ryan stated, "I'm optimistic that will happen again this year. We don't know the end of the year yet."

  • O'Reilly provided guidance on margins, noting, "We expect [adjusted EBITDA margin] to continue to improve. Where we see that going is to a kind of typical SaaS type company profitability, late 20s, early 30s type range."

Financial Results

  • For Q3 2025, revenue was $16.1 million, with SaaS license revenue at $15.2 million. Gross profit reached $12.2 million and gross margin was 76%. Operating expenses rose to $14.3 million, R&D expense net of capitalized R&D was $16.1 million for the first nine months, sales and marketing expense was $17.0 million, and G&A expenses were $9.1 million. The company ended the quarter with $59.8 million in cash.

  • ARR at quarter end was $68.6 million, up 37% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Doug Taylor, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about macroeconomic headwinds and increased competition. Ryan responded that while there has been "an impact there around customer budgets and uncertainty," there is evidence of stabilization, particularly in the U.S. He added, "Kneat is still winning all the key deals... competition has the ability to slow deals down a little bit, but they still -- Kneat is still winning them in general."

  • Erin Kyle, CIBC: Queried about deferrals in customer expansions and FX impacts. Ryan confirmed some deals moved from Q3 to Q4. O'Reilly quantified, "from a quarter-over-quarter perspective on ARR, we have a tailwind of approximately $1 million... the revenue impact is a tailwind of about $130,000."

  • Gavin Fairweather, Cormark: Asked about adoption of agile workflows and R&D strategy. Ryan said, "early customers are using it and giving feedback... it's coming through very strong at the moment, and we're really excited about what it's achieving." On competition, Ryan stated the company is "in a very good place" and expects further innovation to "put further distance between us and the competition."

  • Justin Keywood, Stifel: Inquired about margin targets and capital allocation. O'Reilly projected adjusted EBITDA margins in the "late 20s, early 30s type range" over the next 18 months. Ryan said, "there's no plans to spend any of that balance sheet right now... a strong balance sheet is a very positive thing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on macroeconomic headwinds, deal deferrals, competition, and margin expansion, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions sought clarity on the sustainability of growth and operational leverage.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing robust pipelines, strong customer demand, and ongoing product innovation. Ryan frequently referenced stability and optimism for expansions, while O'Reilly provided precise margin targets. The tone was consistent with prior quarters, with slightly more emphasis on vigilance regarding competition.

  • In comparison to the previous quarter, management's confidence remained steady, while analysts continued to probe on recurring concerns about deal timing and cost structure.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • ARR growth moderated from 43% in Q2 to 37% in Q3, while total revenue growth decelerated from 32% to 26%. Operating expense growth accelerated, with notable increases in R&D and sales and marketing.

  • Margin guidance became more specific, with O'Reilly now targeting a 20–30% range for adjusted EBITDA over 18 months.

  • Strategic focus continues on innovation, AI integration, and maintaining market leadership. Analysts sustained focus on deal timing, competition, and margin improvement.

  • The tone remained confident from management, with an uptick in vigilance regarding competitive entrants.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged extended customer buying cycles and deal slippage due to macroeconomic factors, though these impacts are described as not significant.

  • Increased competition is noted, with specific reference to new entrants such as Veeva, but Ryan stated, "Kneat is still winning all the key deals."

  • Operating expenses are rising, particularly in R&D and sales and marketing, though management frames this as strategic investment for future growth and profitability.

  • Analysts raised concerns regarding deal deferrals, competition, and the sustainability of margin expansion.

Final Takeaway

Kneat.com, inc. emphasized continued leadership in digital validation with robust SaaS and ARR growth, an expanding AI-driven product roadmap, and a confident outlook for margin expansion into the 20–30% adjusted EBITDA range over the next 18 months. Management highlighted strategic investments in R&D and go-to-market as levers for future growth, while maintaining vigilance amid a more competitive landscape and persistent macroeconomic headwinds. The company closed the quarter with a strong cash position, setting the stage for ongoing product innovation and a focus on profitability into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BNY rolls out money market fund for stablecoin providers
data.description:
BNY (BK) is launching a money market fund, called the BNY Dreyfus Stablecoin Reserves Fund (BSRXX), specifically tailored to stablecoin issuers, it said on Thursday.

The new fund is designed to hold the reserves for stablecoins to be issued under the recently signed Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins (GENIUS) Act. The fund does not invest in stablecoins.

As part of the launch, the BSRXX has secured an investment from Anchorage Digital, a crypto platform that lets institutions transact in digital assets. 

BNY Investments Dreyfus is the affiliated liquidity solutions provider of BNY (BK) and the flagship offering on BNY's Liquidity Direct platform. It supports stablecoin issuers by providing regulated money market funds as eligible reserves.

The GENIUS Act, enacted in July, established a federal regulatory framework for dollar-backed stablecoins, specifying the assets that are eligible for stablecoin issuers to hold as reserves. BSRXX can be used by stablecoin providers to hold their reserves once the legislation is effective.

The move comes amid a growing list of major financial institutions using blockchain technology and digital assets to modernize traditional financial mechanisms, all under the Trump administration's pro-crypto push. On Wednesday, for instance, Franklin Templeton (BEN) reportedly joined the bank-backed Canton Network to expand institutional access to its tokenized money market fund.

In October, BNY (BK) was said to have started testing  tokenized deposits -- blockchain-based representations of commercial bank money -- for real-time, cross-border payments.

data.title: Activist investor targets Cracker Barrel's CEO, board member for $1B market value loss
data.description:
Cracker Barrel’s controversial rebranding, which has resulted in a 54% plunge in its share price, has prompted activist shareholder Sardar Biglari of Biglari Capital to call for the removal of CEO Julie Masino and board member Gilbert Davila, holding them accountable for the destruction of more than $1 billion in market value.  

In a letter to Cracker Barrel (CBRL) shareholders, Biglari – who owns a 3% stake in the company -- believes that “only a new CEO with relevant turnaround experience in company-operated restaurants can fix the business and reverse the massive losses that Cracker Barrel shareholders have suffered under CEO Julie Masino” which have led to high levels of short interest, share price decline, and loss of investor confidence.

Proxy advisory firms Glass Lewis and Institutional Shareholder Services ((ISS)) are also advising shareholders to vote against board member Davila, as well as Jody Bilney, the chair of the company’s nominating and corporate governance committee but remain supportive of CEO Masino.

Ahead of the November 20 shareholder meeting, Cracker Barrel (CBRL) acknowledged the “challenges related to a new logo and more modern store design,” but view Biglari’s “distracting” proxy contest as part of a “long history of disruptive, failed campaigns against Cracker Barrel” and that his “false and misleading statements” intend to prolong distraction, further disrupt the business, and to “advance his own interests.”

“The Cracker Barrel board is highly qualified, engaged, and committed to serving the best interests of ALL shareholders…and the removal of Masino would set the company back years, destabilize the business, and delay our return to the momentum of FY25,” the company said in a statement filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

While the board may be behind Masino, the share price suggests otherwise. Since August, Cracker Barrel (CBRL) shares have steadily eroded, landing at an all-time low of $28.60 on Tuesday.

data.title: Planet Fitness rallies after issuing strong guidance at its Investor Day event
data.description:
Planet Fitness (PLNT) is holding its Investor Day event on Thursday in Boston, Massachusetts. The leadership team plans to provide an update on the company's strategic imperatives designed to drive its long-term growth ambitions,

Looking ahead, Planet Fitness (PLNT) wants to evolve and modernize the brand while staying true to the fundamental values that define and differentiate its offerings. Another goal is to enhance the member experience to foster brand loyalty and connection that translates into long-term member growth and captures the increasing fitness market opportunity. Plans are also in place to refine Planet Fitness' club floor plans and amenities to enhance franchise returns as they open, operate, and remodel clubs. Globally, Planet Fitness (PLNT) expects to accelerate new club growth globally by driving topline growth and optimizing club formats to further enhance unit economics.

In terms of guidance for FY26-FY28, Planet Fitness (PLNT) sees low double-digit percent CAGR for revenue and mid-single-digit growth for system-wide same club sales. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to increase at a mid-teens percent CAGR.

"This is an exciting time for Planet Fitness, with several catalysts for growth coming together. The demand for fitness is rising globally, real estate availability is beginning to ease, our brand is evolving in ways that attract all generations and fitness levels, and we are advancing our strategies to accelerate growth," summarized CEO Colleen Keating. "We are excited about the future of Planet Fitness and our ability to deliver exceptional value for our franchisees, members, and shareholders," she added.

Jefferies analyst Randal Konik already weighed in on the Planet Fitness update. "These targets reinforce PLNT’s position as one of the most stable, defensive, and profitable growth models in the consumer space, delivering sustained double-digit top-line growth and mid-to-high teens earnings growth," he updated. "As shares continue to trade near trough levels, we remain strong buyers," he added.

Shares of Planet Fitness (PLNT) rallied 6.2% in late morning trading and are up more than 12% on a year-to-date basis. Short interest stands at 7.2% of the total float.

data.title: Celestica's stock tumbles, while broader market also sees red
data.description:
Shares of Celestica (CLS) fell about 12% on Thursday while the broader market also saw red.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell around 1.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) declined around 0.75%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) had dipped about 0.3%.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analyst Oakoff Investments downgraded Celestica's stock to Hold from Buy due to limited upside and stretched valuation after a strong rally.

Year-to-date, Celestica's stock has surged about 223%.

The analyst noted that Celestica's business momentum remains robust, driven by rapid growth in its High-Performance Solutions, or HPS, sub-segment and expanding margins.

"Despite bullish fundamentals and AI-driven demand, CLS's margin expansion is nearing its natural limit, capping further multiple expansion." said Oakoff Investments. "At current prices, CLS offers only about 1-2% upside, making it fairly valued and warranting a more cautious stance for new investors."
